quotes.quote: Whether the code succeeds or fails, a self-reflection is produced based on the experimental results or the encountered error signal (Renze & Guven (2024); Shinn et al. (2024)). Here, the mle-solver is prompted to reflect on the outcome of its actions. If the program failed to compile, the solver reflects on how to fix this issue in next iterations. If it successfuly compiles and returns a score, the solver will reflect on how to increase this score.
quotes.rationale: Identifies an internal self-reflection evaluator using execution outcomes or errors to affect later iterations.

quotes.quote: Instead of using an LLM scoring function, the mle-solver score is evaluated on the dev set, which is a 20% random sample taken from the original training set, and the training set is represented by the other 80% split.
quotes.rationale: Identifies the program-score channel as a dev-set benchmark metric rather than an LLM scoring function.

quotes.quote: Whether the code succeeds or fails, a self-reflection is produced based on the experimental results or the encountered error signal (Renze & Guven (2024); Shinn et al. (2024)). Here, the mle-solver is prompted to reflect on the outcome of its actions. If the program failed to compile, the solver reflects on how to fix this issue in next iterations. If it successfuly compiles and returns a score, the solver will reflect on how to increase this score.
quotes.rationale: Shows that the returned score is routed into later mle-solver improvement iterations.

quotes.quote: Based on these scores, the PhD agent then decides whether to finalize the project or revisit earlier subtasks—such as planning, experimentation, or results interpretation—to address the feedback. This allows the agents to refine the research report until it meets sufficiently high standards, effectively simulating the real-world academic revision process.
quotes.rationale: Identifies a paper-review score channel whose outputs control finalization or revisiting earlier subtasks.

diagnostics.consequence: Claims about autonomous discovery should be bounded to execution of a human-provided idea, not origination or validation of that idea.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: Unlike previous approaches, where agents participate in their own research ideation independent of human input (Baek et al. (2024); Lu et al. (2024b)), Agent Laboratory is designed to assist human scientists in executing their own research ideas using language agents. Agent Laboratory takes as input a human research idea and outputs a research report and code repository produced by autonomous language agents

diagnostics.statement: The system boundary starts from a human-provided research idea rather than autonomous research ideation.

diagnostics.consequence: The source supports closed feedback for code iterations and paper-revision stage transitions, but not persistent memory or cross-project learning.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: Whether the code succeeds or fails, a self-reflection is produced based on the experimental results or the encountered error signal (Renze & Guven (2024); Shinn et al. (2024)). Here, the mle-solver is prompted to reflect on the outcome of its actions. If the program failed to compile, the solver reflects on how to fix this issue in next iterations. If it successfuly compiles and returns a score, the solver will reflect on how to increase this score.

Based on these scores, the PhD agent then decides whether to finalize the project or revisit earlier subtasks—such as planning, experimentation, or results interpretation—to address the feedback. This allows the agents to refine the research report until it meets sufficiently high standards, effectively simulating the real-world academic revision process.

diagnostics.statement: Internal evaluations directly alter later code iterations and workflow stage transitions.

diagnostics.consequence: Reliance on internal evaluators cannot be treated as established validator reliability, novelty discrimination, bias testing, or probabilistic calibration.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: Whether the code succeeds or fails, a self-reflection is produced based on the experimental results or the encountered error signal (Renze & Guven (2024); Shinn et al. (2024)). Here, the mle-solver is prompted to reflect on the outcome of its actions. If the program failed to compile, the solver reflects on how to fix this issue in next iterations. If it successfuly compiles and returns a score, the solver will reflect on how to increase this score.

Based on these scores, the PhD agent then decides whether to finalize the project or revisit earlier subtasks—such as planning, experimentation, or results interpretation—to address the feedback. This allows the agents to refine the research report until it meets sufficiently high standards, effectively simulating the real-world academic revision process.

Instead of using an LLM scoring function, the mle-solver score is evaluated on the dev set, which is a 20% random sample taken from the original training set, and the training set is represented by the other 80% split.

diagnostics.statement: The packet reports no reliability study for the internal self-reflection, dev-set score routing, or paper-review revision gate.

diagnostics.consequence: The implementation cannot be coded as autonomous repository-level code management; repository modification remains a reported limitation and future-work item.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: Agent Laboratory is also not able to manage repository-level code on its own, but rather the appropriate files are provided to it at each necessary step and files are saved based on which phase produced the file. Enabling flexible repository-level file modification and execution is a clear next step for future work.

diagnostics.statement: Autonomous repository-level code management is explicitly unsupported in the reported implementation.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: Whether the code succeeds or fails, a self-reflection is produced based on the experimental results or the encountered error signal (Renze & Guven (2024); Shinn et al. (2024)). Here, the mle-solver is prompted to reflect on the outcome of its actions. If the program failed to compile, the solver reflects on how to fix this issue in next iterations. If it successfuly compiles and returns a score, the solver will reflect on how to increase this score.

Based on these scores, the PhD agent then decides whether to finalize the project or revisit earlier subtasks—such as planning, experimentation, or results interpretation—to address the feedback. This allows the agents to refine the research report until it meets sufficiently high standards, effectively simulating the real-world academic revision process.

Instead of using an LLM scoring function, the mle-solver score is evaluated on the dev set, which is a 20% random sample taken from the original training set, and the training set is represented by the other 80% split.

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: Report channel-specific error rates or bias patterns, not only aggregate workflow success.
success_check.evidence_needed: A study comparing self-reflection fixes, dev-set score improvements, and paper-review gate decisions against held-out or human-audited outcomes.
success_check.observable: Measured false-positive, false-negative, and systematic error rates for each internal validator channel.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: Unlike previous approaches, where agents participate in their own research ideation independent of human input (Baek et al. (2024); Lu et al. (2024b)), Agent Laboratory is designed to assist human scientists in executing their own research ideas using language agents. Agent Laboratory takes as input a human research idea and outputs a research report and code repository produced by autonomous language agents

Agent Laboratory is also not able to manage repository-level code on its own, but rather the appropriate files are provided to it at each necessary step and files are saved based on which phase produced the file. Enabling flexible repository-level file modification and execution is a clear next step for future work.

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: Documentation explicitly distinguishes human-provided idea input and provided-file execution from unsupported autonomous ideation or repository-level management.
success_check.evidence_needed: Updated system boundary statement and capability matrix tied to implementation traces.
success_check.observable: Capability documentation that lists idea origination and repository-level code management outside the implemented autonomous scope.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: Unlike previous approaches, where agents participate in their own research ideation independent of human input (Baek et al. (2024); Lu et al. (2024b)), Agent Laboratory is designed to assist human scientists in executing their own research ideas using language agents. Agent Laboratory takes as input a human research idea and outputs a research report and code repository produced by autonomous language agents

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: Every accepted input idea has a documented prior-art search result before experimentation begins.
success_check.evidence_needed: A logged prior-art corpus search and human or independently validated novelty decision for the input research idea.
success_check.observable: Traceable novelty-screening record linked to the human-provided research idea.

memory_structure: self-reflection within mle-solver iterations based on experimental results or error signals
persistent memory across separate research projects not_reported
